Summary: PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade provides significant clinical benefits for melanoma patients and induces a high rate of anti-melanoma response, but advanced metastatic melanoma shows a high rate of innate resistance (60–70%). In pretreatment melanoma biopsies, exome and transcriptome sequencing identify potential determinants of response to anti-PD-1, and neoepitopes derived from somatic non-synonymous mutations are indicated as critical for deriving clinical benefit. Responding tumors were enriched for BRCA2 mutations, and higher overall somatic mutational load associated significantly with improved survival after anti-PD-1 therapy. Overall somatic mutational loads were not significantly higher in responsive tumors than in non-responsive tumors. Objective tumor responses strongly associated with survival benefits but were not overwhelmingly driven by overall mutational loads. A relatively low mutational load did not preclude a tumor response, and overall somatic or predicted neoepitope loads of pretreatment tumors are not sufficient to predict response patterns to anti-PD-1 therapy. Innately resistant tumors display the IPRES transcriptional signature, which denotes up‑expression of genes regulating mesenchymal transition, cell adhesion, extracellular matrix remodeling, angiogenesis, and wound healing. MAPK-targeted therapy induces similar signatures in melanoma, suggesting non‑genomic MAPKi resistance mediates cross-resistance to anti‑PD‑1 therapy. Validation across independent tumor cohorts defines IPRES across advanced cancers and suggests attenuating IPRES-related processes may improve anti‑PD-1 response.
Reference: Hugo, W., Zaretsky, J. M., Sun, L., Song, C., Moreno, B. H., Hu-Lieskovan, S., Berent-Maoz, B., Pang, J., Chmielowski, B., Cherry, G., Seja, E., Lomeli, S., Kong, X., Kelley, M. C., Sosman, J. A., Johnson, D. B., Ribas, A., & Lo, R. S. (2016). Genomic and Transcriptomic Features of Response to Anti-PD-1 Therapy in Metastatic Melanoma. Cell, 165(1), 35-44. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2016.02.065

Summary: Systematic elaboration. Tumor cell surface PD-L1 binds PD-1 through its extracellular domain. Melanoma cells release PD-L1–positive extracellular vesicles into the tumor microenvironment and circulation. Those extracellular vesicles have the same membrane topology with an exposed extracellular domain and bind PD-1 in a concentration-dependent manner; PD-L1 blocking antibodies disrupt that binding. Interferon-γ treatment of melanoma cells increases exosomal PD-L1 levels and correspondingly increases exosome binding to PD-1. Extracellular vesicle PD-L1 primarily binds PD-1 on antigen-experienced CD8 T cells that secrete IFN-γ. The exosomal PD-L1–PD-1 interaction inhibits those CD8 T cells and anti-PD-L1 or anti-PD-1 antibodies can block this inhibition. In patients with metastatic melanoma receiving anti-PD-1 therapy, circulating exosomal PD-L1 levels change during treatment and the magnitude of early on-treatment increases stratifies clinical responders from non-responders. Consequently, exosomal PD-L1 enables tumors to counteract immune pressure at the effector stage and may reflect the dynamic interplay between tumor and immune cells.
Reference: Chen, G., Huang, A. C., Zhang, W., Zhang, G., Wu, M., Xu, W., Yu, Z., Yang, J., Wang, B., Sun, H., Xia, H., Man, Q., Zhong, W., Antelo, L. H., Dong, H., Zhao, Y., Karakousis, G. C., Mitchell, T. C., Schuchter, L. M., Herlyn, M., Wherry, E. J., Xu, X., & Guo, W. (2018). Exosomal PD-L1 contributes to immunosuppression and is associated with anti-PD-1 response. Nature, 560(7718), 382-386. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-018-0392-8

Summary: The PD-1–PD-L1 pathway is identified as important for treating some cancers, and signals of clinical activity have been observed in patients with melanoma who were treated with an anti–PD-1 antibody. The anti–PD-1 antibody showed PD-1–receptor occupancy on circulating CD3+ T cells (median 64–70% by dose level after one cycle in 65 patients with melanoma treated at 0.1–10.0 mg/kg every 2 weeks). These signals of clinical activity and the biomarker-based selection approach need prospective assessment. Phase 2 trials with immunologic and molecular-marker correlates are under way. Phase 3 studies of anti–PD-1 antibody for melanoma are being planned. One patient with melanoma who received 3.0 mg/kg of anti–PD-1 antibody had a complete response. Median time to peak concentration was 1 to 4 hours after infusion start, and pharmacokinetics were linear with dose-proportional increases across the 0.1–10.0 mg/kg range. Median PD-1–receptor occupancy on circulating CD3+ T cells was 64–70% by dose level after one cycle (0.1–10.0 mg/kg every 2 weeks) in 65 patients with melanoma.
Reference: Topalian, S. L., Hodi, F. S., Brahmer, J. R., Gettinger, S. N., Smith, D. C., McDermott, D. F., Powderly, J. D., Carvajal, R. D., Sosman, J. A., Atkins, M. B., Leming, P. D., Spigel, D. N Engl J Med, 366(26), 2443-2454. https://doi.org/10.1056/nejmoa1200690

Summary: Of the 19 T cell subsets identified, only two were significantly expanded in ICB treated tumors compared to normal donor blood. One expanded subset was CD8 CD45RO+ PD-1+ TBET+ EOMES+. The CD8 subset is analogous to an exhausted-like terminally differentiated CD8 T cell subset identified in murine tumor models as important for tumor rejection. The other expanded subset was CD4 CD45RO+ ICOS+ PD-1lo TBET+. The CD4 subset is analogous to an activated Th1-like CD4 effector subset that expands in response to CTLA-4 blockade but not PD-1 blockade in murine models. In human melanoma and murine tumor models, anti-PD-1 predominantly induces expansion of specific tumor-infiltrating exhausted-like CD8 T cell subsets. The findings indicate that checkpoint blockade targets only particular tumor-infiltrating T cell populations and that anti-PD-1–induced immune responses are driven by cellular mechanisms distinct from those engaged by anti-CTLA-4.
Reference: Wei, S. C., Levine, J. H., Cogdill, A. P., Zhao, Y., Anang, N. A. S., Andrews, M. C., Sharma, P., Wang, J., Wargo, J. A., Pe'er, D., & Allison, J. P. (2017). Distinct Cellular Mechanisms Underlie Anti-CTLA-4 and Anti-PD-1 Checkpoint Blockade. Cell, 170(6), 1120-1133.e17. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2017.07.024

Summary: In patients with melanoma, anti–programmed death 1 (PD-1) therapy produces objective responses, and approximately 75% of these responses are durable and last for years. Defects in pathways involved in interferon-receptor signaling and in antigen presentation were associated with acquired resistance to PD-1 blockade immunotherapy in patients with melanoma. Approximately 25% of patients with melanoma who had an objective response to PD-1 blockade experienced disease progression at a median follow-up of 21 months. Delayed relapses have been noted long after initial objective tumor regression despite continuous therapy.
